revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:平地建物]]
'''Ground-level Building''' (Heichitatemono) is a style of {{ill|Building|en|Building|qid=Q41176|lt=Building|1=Building}} in which the construction uses the natural ground surface – or ground that has been only slightly embanked – as its {{ill|Floor|en|Floor|qid=Q217164|lt=Floor|1=Floor}}. In the archaeology of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, it is a concept used as a classification based on the level of the floor of the building’s {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|en|Feature (archaeology)|qid=Q814254|lt=Archaeological Feature|1=Feature (archaeology)}}. When a structure served as a {{ill|Apartment|en|Apartment|qid=Q188507|lt=Residence|1=Apartment}}, it is sometimes referred to as a '''Ground-level (type) Residence''' (Heichijūkyo) {{Sfn|石野|2006|pp=10-62}}.
==Overview== It is used as a term to distinguish structures such as {{ill|Pit-house|en|Pit-house|qid=Q940435|lt=Pit-house|1=Pit-house}} (pit dwellings), in which the floor is set below the natural ground surface by excavating into the earth, and {{ill|Raised-floor Building|de|aufgeständertes Gebäude|ja|高床建物|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised-floor Building|qq=}}, which – among the {{ill|Post in ground|en|Post in ground|qid=Q1695743|lt=Pit-post Buildings|1=Post in ground}} – have their floors constructed above the ground surface {{Sfn|文化庁文化財部記念物課|2013|p=192}}. Since the designation is based on the criterion that the floor level is at or very near the ground surface, {{ill|Post in ground|en|Post in ground|qid=Q1695743|lt=Pit-post Building|1=Post in ground}}, as well as {{ill|Foundation|de|Fundament|ja|土台|lt=Foundation Building|lt_ja=土台建物|lt_de=Fundamentgebäude|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Foundation|1=Ground sill|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11423103}} and {{ill|Cornerstone Building|de|Grundsteingebäude|ja|礎石建物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cornerstone Building|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 礎石|1=Cornerstone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1133673}} (except for those with raised-floor structures), are in a broad sense also included under “ground-level buildings” {{Sfn|文化庁文化財部記念物課|2013|p=192}}. It is also sometimes called a ground-level '''-style''' building, but<ref>{{Cite web|和書|author=国営吉野ヶ里歴史公園|url=https://www.yoshinogari.jp/ym/episode04/house.html|title=1.住まいと建築|work=弥生ミュージアム|accessdate=2022-10-31}}</ref> in contemporary Japanese archaeology, structures formerly designated as {{ill|Raised-floor Warehouse|de|aufgeständertes Lagerhaus|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|lt=Raised-floor '''style''' Warehouse|lt_ja=高床'''式'''倉庫|lt_de=aufgeständertes Lagerhaus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised-floor Warehouse|qq=}} or {{ill|Pit-house|en|Pit-house|qid=Q940435|lt=Pit-house '''style''' Dwelling|1=Pit-house}} have come to be called simply {{ill|Raised-floor Building|de|aufgeständertes Gebäude|ja|高床建物|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised-floor Building|qq=}} (or raised-floor warehouse) and pit-house (or pit dwelling), respectively. Similarly, the practice of appending the suffix “style” has become uncommon {{Refnest|group=Note|In the 2010s, in the 'Guide to Excavation Survey' published by {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|en|Agency for Cultural Affairs|qid=Q1009519|lt=Agency for Cultural Affairs|1=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}, these building remains were uniformly recorded without the “style” suffix {{Sfn|文化庁文化財部記念物課|2013|pp=117-224}}.}}.
During the {{ill|Paleolithic|en|Paleolithic|qid=Q40203|lt=Paleolithic|1=Paleolithic}} of the Japanese archipelago, people pursued animals for food and led a nomadic life – living in temporary shelters such as {{ill|Tent|en|Tent|qid=Q170544|lt=Tent|1=Tent}} – so that the {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|en|Feature (archaeology)|qid=Q814254|lt=Archaeological Feature|1=Feature (archaeology)}} of structures such as {{ill|Apartment|en|Apartment|qid=Q188507|lt=Residence|1=Apartment}} were not preserved; consequently, confirmed cases of such structures are extremely rare{{Refnest|group=Note|Remains of definitively identified structures from that period have been found in only about 10 instances {{Sfn|堤|2009|pp=67-71}}.}}. However, at the [[:en:Tanamukaihara Site|Tanamukahara Site]] in {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|qid=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|Sagamihara|en|Sagamihara|qid=Q209779|lt=Sagamihara City|1=Sagamihara}} {{ill|Chūō-ku, Sagamihara|en|Chūō-ku, Sagamihara|qid=Q1132074|lt=Chuo Ward|1=Chūō-ku, Sagamihara}}, a building (residential-type structure) featuring 12 {{ill|Posthole|de|Pfostenloch|ja|柱穴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Posthole|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Posthole|qid=Q1742751}} – which is considered a ground-level building – was discovered, confirming that this type of structure existed as early as the Paleolithic {{Sfn|堤|2009|pp=67-71}}. Although the typical dwelling form in settlements from the {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|qid=Q52824|lt=Jomon period|1=Jōmon period}} (when settled life began) through the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} was recognized as {{ill|Pit-house|en|Pit-house|qid=Q940435|lt=Pit-house|1=Pit-house}}, the discovery in 1985 (Shōwa 60) of remains of a ground-level wall-built structure at the Onda Site in {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|qid=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}}, {{ill|Higashiokitama District, Yamagata|en|Higashiokitama District, Yamagata|qid=Q1155520|lt=Higashiokitama District|1=Higashiokitama District, Yamagata}} {{ill|Takahata, Yamagata|en|Takahata, Yamagata|qid=Q772103|lt=Takahata Town|1=Takahata, Yamagata}}, revealed that ground-level buildings coexisted with pit-houses even during the Jomon period {{Sfn|佐原|2005|p=292}}{{Sfn|石野|2006|pp=57-59}}. Furthermore, at the {{ill|Kuroimine Site|en|Kuroimine Site|qid=Q11678001|lt=Kuroimine Site|1=Kuroimine Site}} in {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|qid=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}} and {{ill|Shibukawa, Gunma|en|Shibukawa, Gunma|qid=Q851492|lt=Shibukawa City|1=Shibukawa, Gunma}}, during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|en|6th century|qid=Q8090|lt=6th century|1=6th century}} (late {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}), an {{ill|Volcanic eruption|en|Volcanic eruption|qid=Q7692360|lt=eruption|1=Volcanic eruption}} of {{ill|Mount Haruna|en|Mount Haruna|qid=Q1191690|lt=Mount Haruna|1=Mount Haruna}} buried the settlement instantly under thick {{ill|Volcanic ash|en|Volcanic ash|qid=Q107099|lt=volcanic ash|1=Volcanic ash}}, preserving aspects of daily life (as reflected in the {{ill|archaeological remains|de|Archäologische Überreste|ja|遺構面|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/archaeological remains|1=archaeological contexts|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q114345798}}) without subsequent leveling. At that site, the number of ground-level buildings was overwhelmingly higher – 36 examples compared to 5 {{ill|Pit-house|en|Pit-house|qid=Q940435|lt=Pit-house|1=Pit-house}} and 8 {{ill|Raised-floor Building|de|aufgeständertes Gebäude|ja|高床建物|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised-floor Building|qq=}} – a point that should be considered when examining the building composition and settlement landscape of ancient communities {{Sfn|文化庁文化財部記念物課|2013|p=192}}. Note that in Japanese archaeology the terms Ground-level Building, {{ill|Pit-house|en|Pit-house|qid=Q940435|lt=Pit-house|1=Pit-house}}, and {{ill|Raised-floor Building|de|aufgeständertes Gebäude|ja|高床建物|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised-floor Building|qq=}} are classification names based on the “height” of the floor, defined as follows: # {{ill|Pit-house|en|Pit-house|qid=Q940435|lt=Pit-house|1=Pit-house}}: a structure whose floor is below the ground surface. # Ground-level Building: a structure whose floor is at the natural ground level or raised only slightly by embankment. # {{ill|Raised-floor Building|de|aufgeständertes Gebäude|ja|高床建物|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised-floor Building|qq=}}: a structure in which a floor board is placed on {{ill|dug pillars|de|ausgehöhlte Pfeiler|ja|掘立柱|1=Postholes|2=ja|3=掘立柱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/dug pillars|qq=}}, thereby elevating the floor above the ground surface.
Thus, a building supported by a main pillar driven into the ground – designated as {{ill|Post in ground|en|Post in ground|qid=Q1695743|lt=Pit-post Building|1=Post in ground}} – is classified as a Ground-level Building if its floor was at ground level at the time, and as a Raised-floor Building if it was elevated above the ground. For this reason, when listing detected remains in excavation reports such as the {{ill|Excavation Report|de|Ausgrabungsbericht|ja|発掘調査報告書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Excavation Report|1=archaeological excavation report|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q85882528}}, the Agency for Cultural Affairs has pointed out that describing them with phrases like "'''Pit-post Building and Ground-level Building'''", "'''Pit-post Building and Raised-floor Building'''", or "'''{{ill|Wall-built Structure|de|Mauerbau|ja|壁建ち建物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wall-built Structure|1=Wall‑built Structure|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q120785472}} and Ground-level Building'''" – which juxtapose building names based on differing classification criteria as if they were entirely separate – is meaningless, and that the description should reflect these classification criteria {{Sfn|文化庁文化財部記念物課|2013|p=192}}. ==Footnotes==
===Notes=== {{Reflist|group=Notes}} ===References=== {{Reflist}} ==References== *{{Cite book|和書|last=Ezasaka|first=Terumi|authorlink=江坂輝弥|last2=Serizawa|first2=Chosuke|authorlink2=芹沢長介|last3=Sakazume|first3=Shuichi|authorlink3=坂詰秀一|title=New Japan Archaeology Concise Dictionary|pages=367-368|chapter=Ground-level Residence Ruins|publisher=New Science Company|year=2005|date=2005-05-20|isbn=9784821605118|ref=harv}} *{{Cite book|和書|first=Makoto|last=Sahara|authorlink=佐原真|editor={{ill|Kaneseki|de|Kaneseki|ja|金関恕|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaneseki|1=Hiroshi Kanaseki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q20039461}}・{{ill|Harunari|de|Harunari|ja|春成秀爾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harunari|1=Hideji Harunari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11513502}}|chapter=Dwellings and Villages of the Jomon and Yayoi Periods|title=The Archaeology of Food, Clothing, and Housing|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|series=Makoto Sahara's Works 5|year=2005|date=2005-07-20|isbn=4000271156|ncid=BA72717478|pages=282-307|ref=harv}} *{{Cite book|和書|last=Ishino|first=Hironobu|authorlink=石野博信|title=Tales of Ancient Dwellings (Reprint Edition)|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|series=Historical and Cultural Selection|year=2006|date=2006-10-20|isbn=4642063021|ncid=BA78777927|ref=harv}} *{{Cite book|和書|last=Tsutsumi|first=Takashi|title=Visual Edition: Paleolithic Guidebook|publisher=Shinseisha|series=Series "Learning about Ruins" Supplement Volume 2|year=2009|date=2009-08-25|isbn=9784787709301|ref=harv}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs, Cultural Properties Division, Monument Section|title=Guide to Excavation Survey|chapter=Excavation of Remains|pages=117-224|publisher=Douseisha|series=Settlement Ruins Survey, 2nd Edition|year=2013|date=2013-07-26|isbn=9784886215253|ncid=BB01778935|ref=harv}} ==Related Topics== * {{ill|Pit-house style ground-level building|de|Grubenhausartige Flachbau|ja|竪穴状平地建物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pit-house style ground-level building|1=Pit‑house style ground‑level building|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q119528768}} * {{ill|Pit-house|en|Pit-house|qid=Q940435|lt=Pit-house|1=Pit-house}} * {{ill|Raised-floor Building|de|aufgeständertes Gebäude|ja|高床建物|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised-floor Building|qq=}} ** {{ill|Raised-floor Warehouse|de|aufgeständertes Lagerhaus|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised-floor Warehouse|qq=}} * {{ill|Post in ground|en|Post in ground|qid=Q1695743|lt=Pit-post Building|1=Post in ground}}
[[File:House No. 11 of Tendo City Nishinumata Site Park.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|qid=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}} {{ill|Tendō, Yamagata|en|Tendō, Yamagata|qid=Q385779|lt=Tendo City|1=Tendō, Yamagata}} {{ill|Nishinumata ruins|en|Nishinumata ruins|qid=Q11629013|lt=Nishinumata Site|1=Nishinumata ruins}}’s reconstructed ground-level building (Building No. 11).]] [[File:Toro Site, tatemono.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|qid=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}} {{ill|Shizuoka (city)|en|Shizuoka (city)|qid=Q174691|lt=Shizuoka City|1=Shizuoka (city)}} {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|qid=Q673607|lt=Toro Site|1=Toro (archaeological site)}}’s {{ill|Pit-house style ground-level building|de|Grubenhausartige Flachbau|ja|竪穴状平地建物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pit-house style ground-level building|1=Pit‑house style ground‑level building|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q119528768}}. It is sometimes described as {{ill|Pit-house|en|Pit-house|qid=Q940435|lt=Pit-house|1=Pit-house}}, but strictly speaking it is a ground-level building {{Sfn|江坂|芹沢|坂詰|2005|pp=367-368}}.]] [[File:竪穴式住居の骨組み.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Framework of a pit-house style ground-level building (Fushiya style) ({{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|qid=Q673607|lt=Toro Site|1=Toro (archaeological site)}})]] [[File:田名向原遺跡旧石器時代建物復元模型1.jpg|thumb|250px|Reconstruction model of a Paleolithic ground-level building at the [[:en:Tanamukaihara Site|Tanamukahara Site]] (inside the {{ill|Old Stone Age Question Hall|de|Altsteinzeit-Fragenhalle|ja|旧石器ハテナ館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Stone Age Question Hall|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 史跡田名向原遺跡旧石器時代学習館|1=Historic Site Tana Mukaihara Remains Paleolithic Study Center|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q110937150}})]]
[[ja:平地建物]] [[de:Flachbau]] [[ja:%E5%B9%B3%E5%9C%B0%E5%BB%BA%E7%89%A9]]
<!--Flatland dwellings--><!--Flatland dwellings--> [[Category:Pages with duplicated content]] ==Merged second translation== [[File:House No. 11 of Tendo City Nishinumata Site Park.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|qid=Q125863|lt=Reconstructed Ground-level Building (Building No. 11)|1=Yamagata Prefecture}} {{ill|Tendō, Yamagata|en|Tendō, Yamagata|qid=Q385779|lt=Tendō City|1=Tendō, Yamagata}} {{ill|Nishinumata ruins|en|Nishinumata ruins|qid=Q11629013|lt=Nishinumata Site|1=Nishinumata ruins}}]] [[File:Toro Site, tatemono.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Pit-house Style Ground-level Building|ja|竪穴状平地建物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pit-house Style Ground-level Building|1=Pit‑house style ground‑level building|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q119528768}} at {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|qid=Q673607|lt=Tōro Site|1=Toro (archaeological site)}} in {{ill|Shizuoka (city)|en|Shizuoka (city)|qid=Q174691|lt=Shizuoka City|1=Shizuoka (city)}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|qid=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is sometimes explained as a {{ill|pit-house building|ja|竪穴建物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-house building|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竪穴式住居|qq=Pit-house|2=ja|3=竪穴式住居|1=Pit-house|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q940435}}, but strictly speaking it qualifies as a ground-level building {{Sfn|江坂|芹沢|坂詰|2005|pp=367-368}}.]] [[File:竪穴式住居の骨組み.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Framework of a pit-house style ground-level building (Fushiya style) (at {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|qid=Q673607|lt=Tōro Site|1=Toro (archaeological site)}})]] '''Ground-level buildings''' ({{ill|Flatland dwellings|ja|平地建物|1=Flatland dwellings|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ground-level buildings|qq=}}; へいちたてもの) are a type of {{ill|Building|en|Building|qid=Q41176|lt=building|1=Building}} in which the {{ill|Floor|en|Floor|qid=Q217164|lt=floor|1=Floor}} is established on the ground surface or on a surface slightly raised by embankment {{Sfn|文化庁文化財部記念物課|2013|p=192}}. In Japanese {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|qid=Q23498|lt=archaeology|1=Archaeology}}, the term is used as a classification based on the position of the floor level in the remains of a building. When the building is used as a {{ill|residence|ja|住居|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/residence|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住宅|1=Dwelling|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q699405}}, it is sometimes called a '''ground-level (style) dwelling''' ({{ill|Ground-level dwelling|ja|平地（式）住居|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ground-level dwelling|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; へいちじゅうきょ) {{Sfn|石野|2006|pp=10-62}}.
== Overview == The term is employed to distinguish these structures from {{ill|pit-house buildings|ja|竪穴建物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-house buildings|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竪穴式住居|qq=Pit-house|2=ja|3=竪穴式住居|1=Pit-house|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q940435}} (pit-house dwellings), in which the floor is set below the ground surface by excavating the earth, and from {{ill|raised-floor buildings|ja|高床建物|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor buildings|qq=}} (built on embankments or on posts) where the floor is constructed above the ground surface {{Sfn|文化庁文化財部記念物課|2013|p=192}}. Because the designation is based on the floor being at the same level as the ground surface, {{ill|Post in ground|en|Post in ground|qid=Q1695743|lt=post-built buildings|1=Post in ground}} as well as {{ill|foundation buildings|ja|土台建物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/foundation buildings|1=foundation buildings|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q120997586}} or {{ill|masonry foundation buildings|ja|礎石建物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/masonry foundation buildings|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 礎石|1=Cornerstone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1133673}} (excluding raised-floor structures) are broadly classified as ground-level buildings {{Sfn|文化庁文化財部記念物課|2013|p=192}}.
They are also sometimes referred to as "ground-level (style) buildings," yet in contemporary Japanese archaeology the practice of appending the term “(style)” has largely fallen out of use. For example, structures formerly designated as "raised-floor (style) warehouses" ({{ill|raised-floor building|ja|高床建物|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor building|qq=}}) or "pit-house (style) dwellings" ({{ill|pit-house building|ja|竪穴建物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-house building|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竪穴式住居|qq=Pit-house|2=ja|3=竪穴式住居|1=Pit-house|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q940435}}) are now simply called "raised-floor warehouses" or "pit-house dwellings" respectively, following the uniform notation adopted in excavation reports issued by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|en|Agency for Cultural Affairs|qid=Q1009519|lt=Cultural Affairs Agency|1=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} in the 2010s {{Refnest|group=Note|2010th year 代に{{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|en|Agency for Cultural Affairs|qid=Q1009519|lt=Cultural Affairs Agency|1=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}が発行した『発掘調査のてびき』では、これらの建物遺構は「式」を付けない表記に統一されている{{Sfn|文化庁文化財部記念物課|2013|pp=117-224}}.}}.
[[File:田名向原遺跡旧石器時代建物復元模型1.jpg|thumb|250px|Reconstruction model of a Paleolithic ground-level building at the [[:en:Tanamukaihara Site|Tanamukawahara Site]] (inside the {{ill|Paleolithic Hatena-kan|ja|旧石器ハテナ館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Paleolithic Hatena-kan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 史跡田名向原遺跡旧石器時代学習館|1=Historic Site Tana Mukaihara Remains Paleolithic Study Center|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q110937150}})]]
During the Paleolithic period of the Japanese archipelago ({{ill|Paleolithic|en|Paleolithic|qid=Q40203|lt=Paleolithic period|1=Paleolithic}}), people led a nomadic lifestyle, frequently moving their temporary dwellings such as {{ill|Tent|en|Tent|qid=Q170544|lt=tents|1=Tent}} in pursuit of animals for food. Consequently, remains of their residences and other structures are rarely preserved; in fact, only about ten confirmed examples of building remains from that era have been detected {{Refnest|group=Note|当該時代における確実な建物の遺構とされるものは、10例程度しか検出されていない{{Sfn|堤|2009|pp=67-71}}.}} At the [[:en:Tanamukaihara Site|Tanamukawahara Site]] in {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|qid=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, within {{ill|Chūō Ward|ja|中央区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūō Ward|1=Chūōku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q20652290}} of {{ill|Sagamihara|en|Sagamihara|qid=Q209779|lt=Sagamihara City|1=Sagamihara}}, 12 structural remains—interpreted as ground-level buildings (dwelling-like remains)—have been uncovered, demonstrating that such buildings existed as early as the Paleolithic period {{Sfn|堤|2009|pp=67-71}}.
Although it is generally assumed that from the advent of sedentary life in the {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|qid=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period|1=Jōmon period}} through the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, settlement dwellings were predominantly {{ill|pit-house buildings|ja|竪穴建物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-house buildings|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竪穴式住居|qq=Pit-house|2=ja|3=竪穴式住居|1=Pit-house|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q940435}}, the discovery in 1985 (Showa 60) at the Ondashi Site in {{ill|Takahata, Yamagata|en|Takahata, Yamagata|qid=Q772103|lt=Takahata Town|1=Takahata, Yamagata}}, {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|qid=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}}, of remains of wall-built ground-level buildings ({{ill|wall-built architecture|ja|壁建ち建築|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wall-built architecture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) has revealed that ground-level buildings coexisted with pit-house dwellings even during the Jōmon period {{Sfn|佐原|2005|p=292}}{{Sfn|石野|2006|pp=57-59}}.
Furthermore, at the {{ill|Kuroimine Site|en|Kuroimine Site|qid=Q11678001|lt=Kuroimine Site|1=Kuroimine Site}} in {{ill|Shibukawa, Gunma|en|Shibukawa, Gunma|qid=Q851492|lt=Shibukawa City|1=Shibukawa, Gunma}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|qid=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}, a catastrophic eruption of {{ill|Mount Haruna|en|Mount Haruna|qid=Q1191690|lt=Haruna-san|1=Mount Haruna}} in the first half of the 6th century (late {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}) caused an entire settlement to be instantaneously buried under a thick layer of volcanic ash. As a result, aspects of life (as indicated by building remains) were preserved without later alteration; notably, at this site 5 pit-house buildings and 8 raised-floor buildings were identified alongside a dominant 36 ground-level buildings, a fact that is crucial for reconstructing ancient settlement structures and landscapes {{Sfn|文化庁文化財部記念物課|2013|p=192}}.
Note that in Japanese archaeology the terms {{ill|ground-level buildings|ja|平地建物|1=Flatland dwellings|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ground-level buildings|qq=}}, {{ill|pit-house buildings|ja|竪穴建物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-house buildings|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竪穴式住居|qq=Pit-house|2=ja|3=竪穴式住居|1=Pit-house|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q940435}}, and {{ill|raised-floor buildings|ja|高床建物|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor buildings|qq=}} are defined based on the "height" of the floor surface: # {{ill|Pit-house buildings|ja|竪穴建物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pit-house buildings|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竪穴式住居|qq=Pit-house|2=ja|3=竪穴式住居|1=Pit-house|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q940435}}: Buildings and structures in which the floor surface is below the ground surface. # Ground-level buildings: Buildings whose floor surface is at the same level as the ground surface, or only slightly raised by embankment. # {{ill|Raised-floor buildings|ja|高床建物|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised-floor buildings|qq=}}: Buildings and structures in which the floor is constructed on top of posts ({{ill|dug-out pillars|ja|掘立柱|1=Postholes|2=ja|3=掘立柱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/dug-out pillars|qq=}}), elevating the floor above ground level.
Thus, a structure described as a {{ill|Post in ground|en|Post in ground|qid=Q1695743|lt=post-built building|1=Post in ground}}—that is, a building supported by main pillars erected in the ground—is classified as a "ground-level building" if its floor was at ground level when it existed, or as a "raised-floor building" if the floor was above the ground surface. For this reason, the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|en|Agency for Cultural Affairs|qid=Q1009519|lt=Cultural Affairs Agency|1=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} has noted that when listing building remains in excavation reports, simply juxtaposing terms such as "post-built buildings and ground-level buildings" or "post-built buildings and raised-floor buildings" (or even "wall-built buildings and ground-level buildings") is meaningless unless the differing classification criteria are taken into account {{Sfn|文化庁文化財部記念物課|2013|p=192}}. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Pit-house Style Ground-level Buildings|ja|竪穴状平地建物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pit-house Style Ground-level Buildings|1=Pit‑house style ground‑level building|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q119528768}} * {{ill|Pit-house Buildings|ja|竪穴建物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pit-house Buildings|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竪穴式住居|qq=Pit-house|2=ja|3=竪穴式住居|1=Pit-house|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q940435}} * {{ill|Raised-floor Buildings|ja|高床建物|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised-floor Buildings|qq=}} ** {{ill|Raised-floor Warehouses|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised-floor Warehouses|qq=}} * {{ill|Post in ground|en|Post in ground|qid=Q1695743|lt=Post-built Buildings|1=Post in ground}}
